prompt: I need to decide whether to put the dishwasher on now (half-full) or wait until tomorrow (definitely full). Walk me through how you'd actually think about this, not a framework.
response:
Right now it’s 9:30 p.m. and the dishwasher is half-full.  
I open it, look at what’s in there: two dinner plates, a couple of cereal bowls, three coffee mugs, the cutting board from tonight’s veg prep, and the usual silverware. The bottom rack is basically empty except for a frying pan.  

I ask myself: “If I run it now, what happens tomorrow?”  
- Tomorrow morning we’ll make breakfast—oatmeal bowls, smoothie cups, knives, cutting board again.  
- Lunch will add two more plates, maybe a pot if I cook pasta.  
- By dinner we’ll easily hit a full load, maybe even need to Tetris a bit.  

So if I run it tonight, tomorrow I’ll have an empty dishwasher ready for the new stuff, but I’ll also have run two half-loads in 24 hours. That’s ~9 liters of water and ~1 kWh of electricity each time (I looked at the manual once).  

If I wait, I’ll run one full load tomorrow night. Same total dishes, one cycle. Saves roughly 9 L of water and 1 kWh.  

Noise isn’t a factor—this model is quiet enough that we can sleep through it. Smell isn’t either; nothing in there is super gross, and the door latches tight.  

The only real downside to waiting is that tomorrow morning the sink might pile up a little until we get the breakfast dishes in. I can live with that; we have counter space.  

So I close the door without pressing start, leave the detergent where it is, and decide to wait.
